Description: Iraq invaded Kuwait triggering the Gulf War. Immediate supply disruption fears sent oil prices spiking from ~$17 to $46/barrel within weeks.
Coalition forces launched Operation Desert Storm. Prices initially surged then fell as the war was brief and supply disruptions were contained.
Thai baht devalued triggering the Asian Financial Crisis. Demand for oil collapsed across Southeast Asia pushing Brent from ~$19 to below $10 by end 1998.
OPEC and non-OPEC producers agreed to cut output by 2.1 million bpd. This stabilised the market and began a multi-year recovery above $20/barrel.
Al-Qaeda attacks on the US created deep uncertainty. Oil briefly spiked then fell as recession fears dominated. Prices remained subdued through 2002.
Coalition forces invaded Iraq. Supply fears had already lifted prices during the build-up. Post-invasion prices remained elevated due to insurgency disrupting Iraqi output.
China's rapid industrialisation drove unprecedented global oil demand growth from 2004 onward, underpinning the commodity supercycle and sustained price rise.
Brent crude reached its historical peak of $147.50 driven by peak demand, speculative flows, a weak USD, and tight OPEC spare capacity.
Lehman Brothers filed for bankruptcy, triggering the Global Financial Crisis. Brent crashed from $147 to under $40 by December 2008 – the largest single-year collapse on record.
OPEC extended production cuts agreed in late 2008. Combined with global stimulus packages, this began a sustained recovery throughout 2009.
Mohamed Bouazizi's self-immolation triggered the Arab Spring. Spread to Libya in 2011, directly disrupting ~1.5 million bpd of Libyan output and pushing Brent above $120.
Libya's civil war effectively halted the country's oil exports. Brent rose from ~$100 to a $126 peak in April 2011 before recovering with Saudi Arabia pledging additional supply.
OPEC, led by Saudi Arabia, decided not to cut production despite a global supply glut and falling prices. Brent collapsed from ~$85 to below $50 over the following months.
Iran returned to the oil market after international sanctions were lifted following the JCPOA nuclear deal. Added ~1 million bpd of Iranian crude to global supply.
OPEC and Russia agreed to cut output by 1.8 million bpd in the first major OPEC+ deal. Prices rose from ~$45 to above $55/barrel within weeks.
Trump administration reimposed sanctions on Iran after withdrawing from the JCPOA. Reduced Iranian exports contributed to Brent rising above $80 in late 2018.
Drone attack on Abqaiq and Khurais temporarily knocked out ~5% of global oil supply. Brent spiked over 15% in a single day before recovering as Saudi production was restored.
Talks between Saudi Arabia and Russia collapsed. Saudi Arabia slashed prices and pledged to flood the market. Combined with COVID-19 demand collapse, Brent fell to 20-year lows.
OPEC+ agreed to the largest ever production cut of 9.7 million bpd. This put a floor under prices and began the recovery as demand gradually returned.
WTI May futures briefly turned negative (-$37.63) as storage capacity neared limits. While Brent did not go negative, it fell below $20/barrel – the lowest since 2002.
Mass vaccination programmes began globally, fuelling expectations of demand recovery. Brent rose steadily from ~$50 to $70 by mid-2021.
Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine triggered sweeping Western sanctions on Russian energy. Brent surged above $130 in March 2022 – the highest since 2008.
